flashcards.back: Path Vector Routing is a routing protocol classification that maintains the entire path to a destination network, including the sequence of Autonomous Systems (AS) that traffic will traverse. It combines features of both Distance Vector and Link State protocols.

flashcards.front: How does Path Vector Routing differ from Distance Vector Routing?
flashcards.back: Distance Vector uses only hop count (metric) and shares entire routing table with neighbors. Path Vector maintains the complete AS path list, enabling loop detection and policy-based routing. Distance Vector is prone to count-to-infinity problems while Path Vector prevents loops using AS-PATH.

flashcards.front: How does Path Vector Routing differ from Link State Routing?
flashcards.back: Link State flooding shares link-state advertisements (LSAs) with all routers in the area, requiring each router to compute the shortest path. Path Vector only exchanges routing information with neighboring AS, maintaining path vectors and allowing administrative policies to influence routing decisions.

flashcards.front: What is BGP and why is it classified as a Path Vector protocol?
flashcards.back: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the primary inter-domain routing protocol of the Internet. It is classified as Path Vector because it carries the AS-PATH attribute listing all Autonomous Systems that a route has traversed, enabling loop prevention and policy-based routing.

flashcards.front: What is the AS-PATH attribute in BGP?
flashcards.back: AS-PATH is a mandatory BGP path attribute that contains the sequence of AS numbers through which a route has been advertised. When a BGP speaker advertises a route to an external peer, it prepends its own AS number to the AS-PATH.

flashcards.front: How does BGP prevent routing loops using AS-PATH?
flashcards.back: When a BGP router receives an update, it checks if its own AS number already exists in the AS-PATH. If found, the route is discarded because it would create a loop. This is the primary loop prevention mechanism in Path Vector routing.

flashcards.front: What is the NEXT-HOP attribute in BGP?
flashcards.back: NEXT-HOP is a mandatory BGP path attribute that specifies the IP address of the next-hop router to reach the destination network. For eBGP updates, this is typically the IP address of the external peer that sent the update.

flashcards.front: What is LOCAL-PREF attribute in BGP?
flashcards.back: LOCAL-PREF is a well-known discretionary BGP attribute used for outbound traffic engineering. It indicates the preferred exit point from the local AS to reach a destination. Higher values are preferred (default is 100). It is only sent to iBGP peers.

flashcards.front: What is the MED (Multi-Exit Discriminator) attribute in BGP?
flashcards.back: MED is an optional transitive BGP attribute used to influence incoming traffic into an AS. It tells neighboring AS which entry point is preferred. Lower MED values are preferred. It is only advertised to eBGP peers.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between eBGP and iBGP?
flashcards.back: eBGP (External BGP) runs between routers in different Autonomous Systems. iBGP (Internal BGP) runs between routers within the same AS. eBGP changes the NEXT-HOP attribute while iBGP preserves it.

flashcards.front: List the four BGP message types and their purposes.
flashcards.back: OPEN: Establishes BGP peering session after TCP connection. UPDATE: Advertises or withdraws routes. KEEPALIVE: Maintains peer relationship (60s interval). NOTIFICATION: Reports errors and closes BGP session.

flashcards.front: What is the BGP route selection process (tie-breaker)?
flashcards.back: BGP selects routes in order: highest LOCAL-PREF > shortest AS-PATH > lowest ORIGIN (IGP < EGP < Incomplete) > lowest MED > eBGP over iBGP > lowest IGP metric to NEXT-HOP > lowest router ID.
